summarization.a: A line chart showing the normalized pressure of ligand and precursor species over time after a single dose, t_d = 0.2 s, β_1 = 10^-2.
summarization.b: A line chart showing the normalized pressure of ligand and precursor species over time after a single dose, t_d = 0.4 s, β_1 = 10^-3.

summarization.d: A line chart showing the normalized pressure of ligand and precursor species over time after microdose sequence, β_02 = 10^-3.

a.question: What was the average precursor pressure at the inlet for all experiments?
What does this figure show about the influence of reaction probability on the temporal separation of precursor and by-product signals in QMS measurements?
a.answer: For high reaction probabilities, the precursor reacts rapidly upon entering the reactor, leading to immediate release of reaction by-products and an offset between the by-product signal and the arrival of unreacted precursor. As the reaction probability decreases, this temporal separation diminishes, and precursor and by-product signals increasingly overlap. When a single dose is resolved into a sequence of microdoses, this effect becomes more pronounced: at high reaction probability, early microdoses are dominated by by-product signals followed by an abrupt transition to precursor-dominated signals, whereas at lower reaction probability both signals persist throughout the dosing sequence with a gradual transition.
